content.problemStatement:
You are given a 9×99\times 99×9 Sudoku represented by a square matrix AAA. Some cells are filled with digits (1(1(1 to 9)9)9) and some cells are empty, denoted by 000.

Find whether the given sudoku is a valid sudoku.

A valid sudoku is the one with following rules:


Each row must contain the digits (1(1(1 to 9)9)9) without repetition.
Each column must contain the digits (1(1(1 to 9)9)9) without repetition.
Each of the nine 3×33\times 33×3 sub-boxes that compose the grid must contain the digits (1(1(1 to 9)9)9) without repetition.
Note:


A Sudoku board (partially filled) could be valid but not necessarily solvable.
Only the filled cells need to be validated according to the mentioned rules.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of nine lines of input.

The next 999 lines describe the rows of sudoku. The ithi^{th}ith of these 999 lines contains 999 integers. Some cells are filled with digits (1(1(1 to 9)9)9) and some cells are empty, denoted by 000.




Output Format
For each test case, output 111 if the given Sudoku is a valid sudoku, or output 000 otherwise.
constraints: Each cell value A[i][j] satisfies 0 <= A[i][j] <= 9
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, exactly 9 lines follow. Each line contains 9 integers separated by spaces. The integers are either 0 (representing an empty cell) or a digit from 1 to 9.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing 1 if the given Sudoku board is valid, otherwise output 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: partially filled board follows all Sudoku rules. Test case 2: a completely solved valid Sudoku. Test case 3: the first row contains two '5's, violating the row rule.

solution.approach:
For each test case we check three independent constraints:
1. Row constraint – no digit 1..9 appears more than once in any row.
2. Column constraint – no digit appears more than once in any column.
3. 3×3 sub‑box constraint – the board is divided into nine 3×3 blocks; each block must not contain duplicate digits.
We ignore zeros because they represent empty cells. The easiest way is to use three arrays of 9 integers as bit‑masks. While scanning the board cell by cell, for a non‑zero value d we compute a bit mask (1 << d). If the corresponding bit is already set in the row, column or block mask, the board is invalid. Otherwise we set the bit. The whole board has only 81 cells, so the algorithm runs in O(81) per test case, i.e. O(T) overall, with O(1) extra memory.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer masks are stored.

code.Python3:
import sys
def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        rowMask = [0]*9
        colMask = [0]*9
        boxMask = [0]*9
        ok = True
        for i in range(9):
            for j in range(9):
                v = int(next(it))
                if v == 0:
                    continue
                bit = 1 << v
                b = (i//3)*3 + j//3
                if (rowMask[i] & bit) or (colMask[j] & bit) or (boxMask[b] & bit):
                    ok = False
                rowMask[i] |= bit
                colMask[j] |= bit
                boxMask[b] |= bit
        out.append('1' if ok else '0')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
